One of Martin Scorsese's all time best films with a bone chilling performance from leading man De Niro. De Niro embodies Travis Bickle so well that you feel like you have met this guy in real life at some point or another.

Three decades before Jay-Z’s Empire State of Mind was written, Taxi Driver depicted a radically different image of New York City. Rather than a space of liberation, New York in Taxi Driver acts as an urban hellscape devoid of a symbolic order that might offer solace to Travis, the film’s protagonist, suffering from the traumas of war, unable to reintegrate into society. He drifts ceaselessly and aimlessly through the night. One can imagine Travis being driven mad with anxiety and paranoia in the formal society of New York, with its yuppies in three-piece suits, monotonous office facades which he attempts to but fails miserably to inhabit, and the ever-visible presence of the state (flags, campaign media, police). Only in night, where the city is gone, leaving only its dirty husk, does the visceral madness subside, and a deeper alienation surface: lack, in the most raw and complete sense, of connection and drive.
The mood, sound, storytelling, and even the lingering feeling after the movie ended were all incredible. Without a doubt, I would give this movie a 5 out of 5.

I'd say it's definitely worth 2 hours if you're the type of person who likes drama and characters with moral ambiguity. I thought it would be a movie that aged poorly when I started watching, but I think the commentary that can be made on mental health and alienation makes the movie better today. Also, most of the criticism that the movie gets is what makes it special because seeing all the flaws of every character as well as 70s NYC is what causes the extreme situations and moral conversation of the film.

I have so much to say about this movie. I hated it and it was a masterpiece. Scorsese builds suspense in a way that’s almost cruel. The moment Travis thinks he’s picking up Betsy, and the camera refuses to pan to the back seat for what feels like four minutes, is brilliant. The tense music, the drifting daydream, the slow realization that someone else is in the car — it’s perfect. The same goes for the TV-tilt scene. The way it wobbles in limbo, neither falling nor settling, mirrors Travis’s mind. When it finally tips over, it feels like the moment he breaks. Travis might be one of the greatest antiheroes ever written. He’s a terrible, weak-minded man with no real thoughts or convictions of his own. He’s the hollow outline of a man without purpose. What infuriates me is that, by the end, he’s celebrated. Even Betsy comes back, almost admiring him. The movie feels like a commentary on how easily society elevates the wrong kind of person. Watching him as someone who works hard to be the right kind of good makes it maddening. There’s a surreal quality to the final stretch, almost like the whole thing might be Travis’s fantasy — a dream of being recognized because he has nothing else to anchor himself to. And that ambiguity is part of what makes the movie both brilliant and deeply uncomfortable.

Obviously, everyone who likes movies should see it. But it's definitely one of those movies people love to say they watched, but didn't really. You know, for false bragging rights. But when I watched it, I actually understood it, unlike some of Scorsese's other movies I've seen. And it doesn't have an excessive runtime.

Taxi Driver is nothing short of a masterpiece, one of the greatest films ever made. Scorsese’s direction, combined with Robert De Niro’s unforgettable performance, crafts a dark, searing portrait of urban alienation that remains unmatched decades later. Its exploration of obsession, loneliness, and moral decay is timeless, and every frame pulses with meticulous artistry. Arguably Scorsese’s finest work, Taxi Driver is a film even its own director cannot surpass — a perfect synthesis of narrative, style, and raw psychological intensity. Bold, uncompromising, and unforgettable, it is cinema at its absolute pinnacle.

Honestly, I have no idea how this became a classic. This movie is nearly unwatchable. It does seem to have a good gritty feel to it. The cinematography is outstanding, especially for the time period. But it’s extremely boring really weird and awkward. The main action scene at the end is horrible. This is just a check the block movie to say Yep I seen the classic that shouldn’t be a classic….

Despite having a lot of controversy, Martin Scorsese’s Taxi Driver brings a lot to the gangster genre.

Its unrelenting intensity might overwhelm some, but for me, it’s perfection. Scorsese’s masterful direction, Paul Schrader’s incisive script, and the cast’s raw power make every frame electric. Taxi Driver is a profound, visceral character study and a searing societal critique, a must-see that lingers long after the credits roll.

Watching this film for the first time, I understood the meaning of the term "masterpiece." While watching it, I was bored, not paying much attention, and just waiting for it to end. The next day, I found myself on the train listening to the soundtrack and rewatching scenes over and over again. For over a week, I couldn't get that film out of my head; it was stuck there and wouldn't go away. At that moment, I realized that this film was the best I'd ever seen. It had impacted me personally like nothing else. I watched it again years later, one morning before going to university, and that was the definitive confirmation: this was my favorite film, but this time, it entertained me every second.

Scorsese and cinematographer Michael Chapman create a funky, overripe New York that's like a nightmare Travis can never stop dreaming... But first and last Taxi Driver belongs to Robert De Niro, the most remarkable young actor of the American screen.

A haunting portrait of loneliness and urban decay. New York becomes a mirror of Travis' mind — and Travis reflects the city's own decay.

Taxi driver stands as a remarkable cinematic achievement that continues to resonate with contemporary audiences. Even a fourteen-year-old viewer, despite the film’s age, can be deeply moved by its poignant portrayal of loneliness and the protagonist Travis Bickle’s relentless quest for fulfillment amidst his profound isolation.

Definitely one of the most notable psychological thrillers out there. Unfortunately its one of those films that is very misunderstood by a large portion of the audience. You'll find reviews saying how Robert De Niro's character is a creep and that the plot-line involving a 12 year old prostitute is disturbing---and both those things are true, since they're very much the points of the film. Robert De Niro is totally a creep in this and that's what makes this movie what it is. Heavily recommend it for anyone interested.

With the near perfect acting performance from Robert De Niro, the incredible writing of Paul Schrader, and amazing cinematography from Michael Chapman it's hard to argue that this isn't one of the greatest films of all time, and a masterclass for filmmakers!

Memorable performances throughout, particularly from Robert De Niro as a man who's lost but through the depth of existence as an NYC cab driver traces an unforgettable route of realization and discovery. Scorsese's taught direction and the wonderful support of Jodie Foster and Harvey Keitel among many others assist in this riveting ride of a man finding salvation within madness. A must-see for certain.

A captivating story about a New York cabbie. Stories like these dive into a realistic day to day living in NYC, Robert De Niro demonstrates an incredible job doing exactly that. The plot of the movie is very realistic with a sudden twist that makes the story hard to navigate. The best part of that is that it really helps you realize by the end the way of thinking Travis was feeling. The overwhelming feeling of loneliness makes you long for purpose, and sometimes you live long enough that the purpose you long for can make you a hero
